content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Horror Movies That'll Genuinely Fuck Me Up?

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Trying to find a horror movie that'll legitimately get me terrified and dreading the dark for days on end (yes I want to do that to myself), because I'm having trouble finding them, please I really need help here. In regards for what I'm looking for: I DESPISE audio stingers in horror, they feel like a laugh track in a sitcom for me, like it's just telling me to be afraid, it just takes me out of immersion and reminds me I'm watching a movie- so preferably not a film with a lot of violin stings, or excessive jumpscares. I did always find the quiet of a lot of horror scenes more frightening than the actual payoff. Secondly, even tho what scares you is subjective, I didn't find the following movies to have a lasting impact of fear on me, or much fear at the time: Gonjiam, Shutter (Original), Hellhouse LLC, Incantation, Lake Mungo, Talk To Me. Finally, not necessary tho, I would prefer movies which aren't recommended at every corner. I just know too much about them (or have already watched them) and they'll feel too safe/expected, stuff like Skinamarink, Weapons, Insidious, Hereditary, The Strangers, Poughkeepsie Tapes, Martyrs, etc. Thank you in advance for recommendations and I hope I didn't come off as pretentious, or arrogant or anything.

Might be helpful but this youtube channel called User 9472 did leave a fairly lasting impact on me with the images and videos they posted.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eden Lake is the film that fucked me up the most. It actually haunts me, years later.
  - Pleased that this was near the top of the replies. It’s my first recommendation whenever people ask for the most fucked up shit I can think of.
2. It Follows 2014, once you watch it, you will genuinely fear strangers reaching near you
3. Speak No Evil (2022), it's a dutch-danish psychological horror film, it genuinely fucked me up. It has an English remake with the same name ft. James McAvoy, but with a changed ending
  - Thought the English remake was pretty good up until the ending.
    - Yea, without spoiling for others, the ending just didn't pack the punch after all that buildup, still good overall. McAvoy was outstanding
4. [Deadgirl](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/23966-deadgirl) (2008)  
>When high school misfits Rickie and JT decide to ditch school and find themselves lost in the crumbling facility of a nearby abandoned hospital, they come face-to-face with a gruesome discovery: a body of a woman stripped naked, chained to a table and covered in plastic and soon realize she is anything but dead. Quickly the boys find themselves embarking on a twisted yet poignant journey testing the limits of their friendship, and forces both to decide just how far they're willing to stretch their understanding of right and wrong.
5. Sounds like you want something that crawls under your skin and stays there. Check out \*\*Caveat\*\* - it's this slow burn Irish horror that builds dread through pure atmosphere and weird imagery instead of cheap scares. The whole thing feels like being trapped in a fever dream and the sound design is genuinely unsettling without relying on jump scare bullshit

  
Also maybe \*\*His House\*\* if you haven't seen it yet - less about scaring you in the moment and more about that lingering "something is very wrong here" feeling that sticks around after
6. When evil lurks and Noroi the curse. I find to truly be affected by horror you have to put away the phone, turn off the lights and fully lock in though.
7. Old Boy

## 2. Looking for movies with cults that control a village/group of people in secret.

**Score:** 25
**Body:** recently watched hot fuzz and im looking for movies with a cult or group are secretly controlling a village or something. could be like night in the woods (game).

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Village
2. Midsommar
3. Wicker man
4. Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom
5. The village
6. The Empty Man
7. Perhaps the British horror classic *”The Wicker Man”* (1973) starring Christopher Lee, Edward Woodward and Britt Ekland is something that you might be interested in watching.
8. check out Apostle, Kill List, The Sacrament, or The Endless.
9. maybe They Live
10. Let’s start a cult

## 3. Movies like Napoleon Dynamite, Sasquatch Gang, and Gentleman Broncos

**Score:** 21
**Body:** All three came out of the Napoleon Dynamite camp and they are all pretty similar in terms of being a nerdy, painfully awkward, funny coming of age. Looking for the closest vibe 

Did not like Eagle vs. Shark

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hot Rod
2. Welcome to the Dollhouse
3. Welcome to the Dollhouse
4. Slums of Beverly Hills 

Welcome to the Dollhouse
5. Dinner in America
6. Ghost World?
7. Did you watch any of the other Jarred Hess moves? I really like Nacho Libre and Masterminds.
8. An Evening with Beverley Luff Lynn is a comedy masterpiece with great performances from some brilliant actors who know how to deliver awkward humour. 100% recommend!
9. Nacho Libre
10. Better Off Dead. And oldies but goodie!

## 4. Looking for Movies Where the Child Actor Is The Heart of The Movie

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Please suggest movies where a child actor is the lead and is the heart of the movie.  But no child prodigy movies, please.  Here are examples of what I'm looking for.

Cinema Paradiso, Giuseppe Tornatore, original version, not Director's Cut.

My Life as a Dog, Lasse Hallström.

A Perfect World, Clint Eastwood.

Leolo, Jean-Claude Lauzon.

Leon: The Professional, Luc Besson.

The Man from Nowhere, Lee Jeong-beom.

The Butterfly, Philippe Muyl.

Nowhere Special, Uberto Pasolini.

Let the Right One in, Tomas Alfredson.

Beasts of the Southern Wild, Benh Zeitlin.

Hunt for the Wilderpeople, Taika Waititi.

Thanks.



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Moonrise Kingdom is perfect for this - Wes Anderson nailed that innocent but determined kid energy and the whole movie revolves around those two kids running away together
2. Little Miss Sunshine

Super 8 

Goonies 

Stand By Me 

E.T
3. Empire of the Sun.  Time Bandits.  The Gate.  The Last Starfighter.
4. 6th Sense
5. My Girl, Paper Moon, Kolya
6. Days of Heaven  

The Florida Project  

The Secret of Roan Inish
7. True Grit. Hailee Steinfeld was a revelation.
8. The Piano, Jane Campion. Ana Paquin won the Oscar for it
9. North

Harriet the Spy

Little Princess (both Shirley temple version and the 1990s remake)

The Secret Garden - I prefer the 90s version.
10. Empire of the Sun

## 5. Civil war movies/shows

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Anyone know any good movies about the civil war? I watched glory but I can’t really find anything else. Or are there any shows about the civil war? kinda like how Band of brothers is about WWII

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Gettysburg is pretty solid if you don't mind a long runtime - it's like 4+ hours but covers the whole battle really well. Ken Burns' Civil War documentary series is also incredible if you're into that style, way more detailed than any movie could be
  - Gettysburg is a great film!
2. Cold Mountain is set during the Civil War. Not necessarily a war movie, but still very good.
3. Ken Burns Civil War
4. Outlaw Josey Wales
5. Free State of Jones (2016)
6. Lincoln movie from Spielberg
7. Gods and Generals is the prequel to Gettysburg. It is long and has its faults, but the music is really good and Stephen Lang’s performance as Stonewall Jackson is outstanding.
8. Andersonville (1996)
9. Cold Mountain (2003) takes place during the Civil War, has one very gnarly scene of a battle that actually took place.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. ‘Fury’ had one of the best foreboding moments. What are some of yours?

**Score:** 1951

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I always liked the aerial view of Juàrez in Sicario, as the convoy are heading to the pick-up point. The soundtrack really adds to the uneasy, foreboding of the scene.
  - I remember that scene and just thinking about the fucking insane amount of military equipment the US has.
  - So much amazing cinematography in that flick.
2. The Nostromo landing on LV426. Can't believe that movie is almost 50 years old.
3. Anyone who's ever been to war understands this scene.
  - The acting in this movie is incredible. The scene at the table with the German women is really something.
    - Its hopeful at first and then horrifying and heartbreaking. Great scene.
      - Gotta give it to Jon Bernthal, I fucking hate his character in it but that’s because his acting is so good in that scene.
4. I rewatched this movie recently and it dawned on me, perhaps it’s obvious to others, that the crew of the tank all represent the skills necessary to survive war. Bible has faith, Grady has the unhinged insanity, Gordo has skills, and Wardaddy exhibits all the traits at once, thus being the leader. Norman is the inexperienced solider that must adopt all these traits in order to adapt to war.
  - Im going to rewatch this and consider what you said while I do.

## 2. One of my favourite scenes in V for Vendetta,the way they executed it was beautiful. And it was deeply profound.

**Score:** 1605
**Body:** There are many things to like in the movie, but this scene stays with you because of how profoundly it was executed.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “I have not come for what you meant to do”.

Edit:  The whole scene is just wonderful. I love the contrast between these two lines.  Her intentions cannot save her, her regrets cannot save her, but her apology is never meaningless.
  - I have not come for what you hoped to do, I've come for what you've done.
2. I love this entire scene - Prothero & Lilliman meet violent ends, but Surridge shows remorse, and even *welcomes* her death. Time has allowed her to dwell on her actions, and though she couldn't stop or undo those actions, nor stand up to the fascist government, she did not contribute further...but she must still die.

It also showed V's interpretation of vegeance & justice; if you profiteered, you must suffer. If you are remoreseful, it is painless. But you must pay for your crimes
  - It mimics the Crow in many ways (at its core, V's journey is very similar to Eric Dravens) in both the movie and the comic in the way that Funboy was the only gang member to at the very least acknowledge and reflect on what he did which allowed him to go the way he chose.
3. *me, a dumb as rocks 13 y/o*: wow this is badass!


*Me, now mid-30s and moderately versed in political theory*: wow this is badass! (And also incredibly intelligent and mature, there's lines borderline lifted directly from revolutionary thinkers & philosophers going back 200ish years)
  - You should try the graphic novel.
4. Touching and powerful. Such a different fate than the others involved. Great scene in an all time great movie.
5. For all the others, their deaths were personal to V and gratifying. He reveled in killing them.

For Surridge, it wasn’t for V at all. It was for the other patients in Larkhill, perhaps for Valerie most of all.
6. Watched this again a couple months ago for the first time in like a decade. Little too close to home now.
  - I remember feeling it was close to home when it came out and we were ass-deep in the post-9/11-PATRIOT-Act Bush Era, this was even before Edward Snowden blew the whistle. My poor little naive teenaged self, oh how I wish you might know how much worse it can get.

## 3. The Most Nominated Film in the 98-Year History of the Oscars

**Score:** 1600
**Body:** Was sinners that good or I just watched the wrong sinners cuz what's the hype about

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For some reason I always thought LOTR was up there
  - Lord of the Rings has the record for wins if you count all three films and Return of the King won every award it was nominated for
    - Yeah the 11/11 is the big record for LOTR, to me.  ROTK has the most of any film (tied with Titanic and Ben-Hur), and the best perfect record of any film.
    - That’s probably what I was thinking then, thank you!
    - Return of the King is tied for the most at 11 awards, even if you include his the one film.
2. Proof that the Oscars shouldn't be taken seriously.
  - Yea especially once it came out last year that the voters don’t even watch all of the nominated movies
    - I believe that has always been the case. People vote for their friends or for completely arbitrary reasons.
3. I enjoyed the film but this is absurd it was no where near as good as people hyped it up to be.
  - Same here. I watched it without knowing anything about it and was pretty surprised when I heard it's apparently viewed as some masterpiece. It was good, entertaining. But I didn't really feel like "wow, this was one of the best movies I've ever seen".

I can see why it would get nominated for best original soundtrack but other than that... eh

## 4. What's an example of a performance that was fantastic in a movie that was bad or that you simply didn't like?

**Score:** 960
**Body:** I just watched Die My Love with Jennifer Lawrence and Robert Pattinson. I didn’t care for the movie at all, it’s just not my thing, but I did think Jennifer Lawrence was excellent. What are some examples that come to mind for you of actors who delivered a really great performance in a movie that was either not received well, or that you personally didn’t care for?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tom Holland in Cherry (2021). He did not get enough credit for his acting in that mediocre film!
2. Raul Julia in Street Fighter. An absolute tour de force performance.
  - And while he was dying too. The man was a master!
    - My ex boss worked on the set of Street Fighter (he had to hook up and maintain all the TV's in Bison's lair) and apparently Raul Julia was really lovely but he had to go back to his trailer immediately after he was done on set every day and just rest and recuperate for as long as possible because he was so unwell while filming.
    - "He took the role because his children loved the franchise and he wanted to star in a film they could enjoy" oof
3. Christian Bale is good in Thor Love and Thunder.
4. Sam Rockwell in Choke. The most explicitly perfect casting…. Terrible movie. Especially when the source material is amazing.
5. While it’s far from a bad movie (personally I think it’s great), Wanted is a movie about assassins who can bend bullets so you know, not exactly Oscar-bait. 


Well, James McAvoy said “fuck it, I’ll still bring my A-game” and delivers such a good performance in Wesley that i will never stop singing his praises. Seriously, go back and rewatch it specifically looking at his acting and he is just phenomenal.
6. Oscar Issac has one really great scene in Sucker Punch, where he plays a evil nightclub manager berating his dancers for trying to leave him. He just comes out of nowhere and absolutely dominates for like 5 minutes. This was just before he really started getting noticed, and I think this scene is part of why.
7. I didn’t dislike the movie exactly, but David Dastmalchian is acting leagues and leagues above everyone else in Late Night with the Devil.

## 5. Am I the only one who has seen this movie? - Southland Tales with Dwayne Johnson, Sarah Michelle Gellar and Justin Timberlake

**Score:** 370
**Body:** I’m still not sure what the story was really about, even today.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Is that 2 stiflers or is one of those dudes actually Justin timberlake?
  - Stifler 2: Electric Boogaloo
  - Scott played twins in this movie. Wild of them to put both characters on the poster.
2. Nobody rocks the cock like Krysta Now!
3. This movie is so weird...it's like a fever dream

100% would recommend.
  - Just as I was watching and thinking it can’t possibly get more bizarre, BOOM! Jon Lovitz.
4. The musical number with Justin Timberlake and the Rockettes is so good.
5. I've never seen this; I just know that it's considered to have dissipated any momentum that its writer-director had coming off cult favorite *Donnie Darko* (for which he is still best known).
  - I think it was the inspiration for the whole arc in Entourage where the director Billy Walsh makes a hot Indy film so they give him a bunch of money to make a big blockbuster and it’s complete shit
6. Scientists are saying the future is going to be far more futuristic than they originally predicted.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The best film of the year is actually Eddington

**Score:** 617
**Body:** And no one wants to have that conversation because the subject matter of the film is so sensitive. But I can’t think of another film in the past couple of years, other than Parasite, that has captured the state of the world at its present moment. It’s insane to think that it wasn’t even nominated for Best Picture, but then it seems all the most of the prior Best Picture winners of the last ten years or so have all seemed to enforce a moral lesson rather than award plain good art. When OBAA is nominated and Eddington is not, you know there is something wrong. OBAA is fundamentally the anti-Eddington in that it appears to choose an ideological side where the latter does not. It’s difficult to not be disaffected when the bias is so evident.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eddington definitely picks a side. Once you realize that the main character is the eldritch horror that is Solidgoldmagikarp and the forces of capital behind it, then it all starts to make sense. All of the stuff that happens on screen is ancillary to the real story that is happening in the background. I think this becomes very clear on second and third viewings.
  - The state of the US is quite sad to me in that you can depict your protagonist as an idiotic, narcissistic, sociopathic murderer, and have him be conservative, and people don't really get the point of it because it's so normalized. 

No doubt that Solidgoldmagikarp is the central, gravitational pull of the film. But I think it's hard to divorce the actions in the foreground of Joe with the actions in the background of the company.
    - Joe (who is undeniably the villain IMHO) wasn't motivated by ideological or political opposition to the datacenter, he was motivated by personal grievances with Ted Garcia. He would have run in opposition to anything Garcia stood for. The specifics didn't matter, he just hated the guy.

Joe's role is defined by his usefulness to capital. The company that tried to kill him flipped on a dime and made him the face of the project after crippling him. The same applied to the natives that had land rights, Cross' mother in law, etc.

Cross and Garcia and everyone else in the town are defined by being subjects and not being able to see that.
  - Yeah I am on board with OP except for that very key misreading of the movie. Like I don't think Eddington could be any clearer in its belief that paranoic right wing violence -- inflamed by big tech -- is going to get us all killed.

Which, given what's happening in Minneapolis, is a pretty crucial thing to be diagnosing right now. Hopefully some day our politicians will be willing to address it 🤪
    - I felt like the film was kind of baiting the audience into criticizing it for taking a "both sides are bad" approach, even though it very clearly takes a side. Even beyond the core thesis that the real enemy is Big Capital,  there's a consistent pattern of 1) setting a progressive viewpoint up as ridiculous, then 2) explicitly validating that viewpoint through later events. E.g. >!anti-police protestors look silly -> police later shown actually abusing their power; white lady looks silly lecturing black cop about racism -> black cop later framed and almost murdered by white colleagues; mask mandates look silly -> covid later shown to be real threat.!<

If you've got twitter brain and you're used to reacting to events sequentially in isolation you have to force yourself to take an extra step back to see how the film's portrayals of different positions interact with each other.
      - He even said in an interview he’s a hard leftist and his “both sides” in the movie is one side is annoying and the other side wants to kill you and destroy the world 
      - Your post reminded me >!how much I laughed at Joe Cross running around with COVID and a machine gun when I saw the movie the second time.!<

Joaquin Phoenix is such a good physical comedian
2. -no one wants to have this conversation

-the 4th highest voted post of the month is about how Eddington was underrated and under appreciated, from 7 days ago

It’s cool that people like Eddington but we need to stop this narrative that you’re the only one smart enough, or special enough, to understand and like Eddington.
3. Best movie of the year to me is No Other Choice. It has the political salience of Eddington, the level of filmmaking and story as OBAA, and better acting than both. It convinces you of rooting for a bad person better than Marty Supreme does. Eddington suffered from this issue immensely. Joe Cross is just not likable enough, so it’s harder to care about his journey. Part of what makes No Other Choice so great is that it tracks the family drama beside the crime drama so clearly. You get to see how good Mansu has it, how the main conflict motivates his arc, and how each choice throughout the narrative escalates his descent. In Eddington, the tracking is not as clear. Joe’s relationship is already strained and Joe’s motivation for his corruption arc is not as clear or convincing. I think No Other Choice just tells a similar story to Eddington but in a more refined way.

That being said, I think Eddington is still a good movie and the way people discuss it is deeply frustrating. I sometimes feel like I’m talking to a character in the film when I talk to people about Eddington.
4. I agree with the sentiment 100% and I think this film will age like fine wine. However, I disagree that Eddington didn’t pick a side. I believe it clearly does, but it’s farrrr more subtle than OBAA

## 2. I marathoned Happiness, A Serbian Film, and Martyrs back to back and I know nothing about film

**Score:** 58
**Body:** Because what else do you do on a snowy Sunday when it's -20 degrees outside and your car wont start?

I've been interested in reading about grindhouse and exploitation films recently. I watched Hobo with a Shotgun when I was like 13 and it scarred me. I researched the movie more than a decade later and thats how I learned what grindhouse films are. I was interested and wanted to watch some more. I made a mini list and today while I was snowed in, decided to watch them back to back. 

From my reading, I heard that A Serbian Film and Martyrs were among the top two with wildly mixed reviews. I was terrified to watch A Serbian Film. I knew nothing about Happiness or Martyrs. Here's how my snow day went:

Happiness (1998): I'm glad I started my marathon with this one. The "comedy" aspect of being a black comedy still came through. The storyline was easy to follow. I genuinely laughed at certain points. It reminded me a lot of Hobo with a Shotgun in parts, with the technicolor absurdity and campy grotesqueness. I liked how the storylines intertwined. 

My takeaway from this film was a reflection on keeping up appearances, toxic family dynamics, and the search for joy, connection, and meaning in the confusing rat race of life. It felt very human, in the most perverse and awkward ways. I was genuinely uncomfortable during the scene where Timmy Maplewood was talking to his dad on the couch. Other than that, the story hit me with an awkward appeal that I genuinely enjoyed watching. 

A Serbian Film (2010): I was afraid to watch this movie from the reviews. I didn't know what I was getting myself into. I did read the plot ahead of time, and brushed up on a very brief explanation of the political conflicts in Serbia to go in with some awareness about what the director claimed he was trying to speak to. That background knowledge helped me connect the dots better and brace myself for the ending. 

For what its worth, I was only able to find the cut/edited version on streaming sites. I dont know if the uncut version would have affected me differently. That said, the first 45-60 minutes of the movie were perfectly fine. I kept wondering why everyone gave it such awful reviews.

Then the sheet/mask scene happened and I was revolted, but my eyes were glued to the screen. Kind of like watching a train wreck happen in slow motion. I was shocked to find out there was still another 15 minutes left of the film after that scene, and those last 15 minutes (up until the last line) are equally horrific.

It was strange to watch a film that was so tame for more than half the movie descend into utter darkness and despair. 

Once I processed the initial shock, I was able to see some things lacking. The storyline was semi-disjointed and hard to follow in parts, even having read the plot summary before I watched it. The shock was overdone to some degree and it lost some of the creeping, horrifying feeling that other slow-burn psychological thrillers have left me with. The plot and characters were all thin.

That being said, it had my mouth hanging open. I dont feel qualified to speak on how well it represents the political struggles of the Serbian people because I'm just some white girl from America, but I can say it was deeply disturbing, and it's even worse confronting the idea that things like child rape and sexual exploitation happen around the world, to varying degrees of depravity, every single day.

Martyrs (2008): I decided to watch the French version as I heard it was better than the newer American version. I will admit, I'm only halfway through (it's getting late and I wanted to make this post before I finished it and fell asleep, plus I needed the break) but even halfway through it strikes me as more artistic than ASF. 

The cinematography is unique, the story line is building layers, and I think it's quite a feat to write a character who the viewer can still be sympathetic towards even after blowing people away with a Shotgun in the first 30 minutes of the film. I'm excited to finish it; so far it seems deeply complex, compelling, and meaningful.


So, that was my snow day! I want to keep watching more movies like this. I know absolutely nothing about film, but I want to change that. Here is the rest of my current watch list, please tell me if these have any merit based on my reviews above and anything else I should watch!

-Pink Flamingos

-A Clockwork Orange

-Salò 

-Dogtooth

EDIT: I finished Martyrs. I...do not have anything intelligent to say. I feel like I can't even digest everything I just watched. I'm physically nauseous and there's a pit in my throat. The arches of my feet and palms of my hands hurt. I need to shower. I don't believe in God but I feel like I should pray? Everyone should watch that movie but I personally never want to see it again. Anything ASF thought it was doing, this movie did it more. GoodNIGHT.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I really wish you had finished Martyrs before you posted lmao. 


You should definitely add Gaspar Noé's Irreversible and Climax to your list.  Also Calvaire, Eden Lake, Funny Games (American version is fine if you prefer, as it's the same movie shot for shot, just in English instead of German), In My Skin, Inside, Rec, and Speak No Evil (both the English and Danish versions - I think the English version is better because James McAvoy kills it, but the Danish version is more fucked and also very good),



Really, just go through the list on Wikipedia's article on New French Extremity. 



Edit: fix language and spelling. 


Edit 2: found this link in another thread of a large collection of NFE 


https://letterboxd.com/ryanismorning/list/new-french-extremity/
  - I watched Climax just the other week. Highly recommend.


But if your recommending Climax, I feel Possession is worth a check out as well
    - That 42 minute single cut scene is incredible, as is everything else in that film, including the soundtrack. I downloaded that and listened to it on runs, and also while drunk at 3am on many occasions, for months.


Even as soon as the opening scene started I knew I was in for something special. Very rare that I rewatch a movie soon after the first time but I watched it three times that week and a few since. I love sharing it with people. 


Possession is loaded into Plex and ready to go. I've just been waiting for the right night  
      - *Climax* is definitely now my comfort film. I can watch it whenever. Like *Jurassic Park* and *To Wong Foo, Thanks For Everything*.
  - I finished Martyrs. I...do not have anything intelligent to say. I feel like I can't even digest everything I just watched. I'm physically nauseous and there's a pit in my throat. The arches of my feet and palms of my hands hurt. I need to shower. I don't believe in God but I feel like I should pray? Everyone should watch that movie but I personally never want to see it again. Anything ASF thought it was doing, this movie did it more. GoodNIGHT.
    - Ironic you saying that about god seeing as the biggest takeaway I had from martyrs was that god is either not there at all or a profoundly horrific entity. The gallery of photos of people who acheived martyrdom is so haunting, stayed with me for weeks after.
2. Martyrs is one of my favorites. How you felt about it you'll feel for Salo. I won't spoil anything about it, I saw Salo recently for the first time and if that was the last movie ever made, that would be a fitting end for cinema. It feels like a conclusion of humanity. I'm not trying to hype it up, it's not a fun experience, it's the most cynical, horrible film but the horror is the point of that. It makes you want to turn away from your love of movies.

It snowed here so I watched some more of Satantango which is a 7 hour meditation on the absurdity of faith. It's not a violent film, although a cat gets killed in an uncomfortable scene that I'm not sure how they filmed (digital trickery would've been my guess but the movie was from 1994 and I can't tell), but anyway it's not like the others on your list, but it should be on your radar. The book version is better imo and I started with reading that.

I also read the book of Clockwork Orange before seeing that but the movie is equal to the book if not better. I used to listen to the soundtrack every morning before school. I don't know if I'd have survived highschool otherwise. It's one of the best soundtracks ever made.

I told my parents Dogtooth was a great family movie and they'd like it because it's Greek (they're Greek). I don't know if they were pretending but sometime later they told me they saw it and enjoyed it. (it is \*not\* a family film btw, I wanted them to see it for obvious reasons if you know what it's about)

OH and Pink Flamingos is fun trash, and I think John Waters would agree. It's the opposite tone of a lot of these on your list. Everything else is much darker.
3. cool writeups! hobo with a shotgun is a classic. idk much about grindhouse but i love edgy flicks that feel confrontational and uncomfortable and transgressive and i love an opportunity to recommend some flicks i can't normally in good conscience recommend to my friends and family!

happiness is lowkey one of my favorite movies. like you said its just so human. the performances are insane too. for more fucked up slice of life, abject realism type shit i'd maybe recommend fire walk with me, possession, irreversible, the piano teacher, inland empire, gummo, clean shaven, man bites dog. 

i haven't seen matryrs but it's been on my list for a while and this does make me interested in seeing it! it sounds badass tbh. as for a serbian film, i saw it once when i was a teenager and all metaphor and context was lost on me, but it did feel purely shocking even then. 

a clockwork orange is another fav, just a really fun fucked up flick, a meditation on morality, violence and free will in the modern age. mad and cartoonish and has a killer soundtrack lol.

pink flamingoes is a treasure and all john waters' stuff makes me glad to be alive in this absurd world !!!

wishing you well on your adventures in movie-watching 👍
4. La Vie Nouvelle and Sombre are pretty miserable experiences. Dancer in the Dark is miserable too but in a completely different way. Maybe check in with Cat Sick Blues, that movie is seriously fucked. Those are some off the top of my head that I haven't seen listed yet.
5. I wouldn't have said any of these are "grindhouse" movies.


Grindhouse as far as it's a genre at all, refers to low-budget theatrical exploitation films churned out between the 60s and early-80s, intended to be shown in grindhouse theatres (cut-price cinemas specialising in sensational content as a strategy to compete with television).


Martyrs and Serbian Film *are* arguably exploitation films, but they're from the wrong era and from a different sort of creative world, with higher production quality. Happiness is just a twisted jet-black comedy from the 90s.


Grindhouse would be stuff like Russ Meyer movies, I Spit on Your Grave, Dolemite, sexploitation, nunsploitation, Mad Max ripoffs, and if you're looking for extreme content, stuff like Faces of Death and Cannibal Holocaust.

## 3. Examples of particularly innovative editing?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I am doing a deep study on editing, primarily for my own work. I’ve gone through a lot of the typical editing books and award-winning films noted for their strong editing, story structure etc.

But what I’m looking for now are examples of particularly creative, crazy, or otherwise unique editing. Film or anime are fine, the more examples the better. Can you recommend some favorites?

Thank you!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m a producer/director who spent about 15 years as an editor before that, and to me, Park Chan-Wook is probably the most innovative mainstream director today with his editing. I didn’t *love* No Other Choice or Decision to Leave overall, but it’s inarguable that he visualises and edits scenes like virtually no one else at the moment.

Even his attention to minute detail in the cuts is there. For example, there’s a cut in No Other Choice where one scene ends, and in a stairway at the back of the shot, leaves start to rustle as a gust of wind blows them. Then the next immediate shot is a car driving through frame, blowing away leaves as it goes. The sound of rustling leaves carries across the two shots.

It’s such a minor, simple detail, that honestly most people may not even notice. But I was so struck by the sheer intentionality of it, triggering a leaf rustle in the background of a shot just to create a match-cut opportunity with the next shot. And not necessarily for any strong thematic or plot reason, just because he can. His films are full of much more bold and obvious editing choices than just this though, so worth seeing.

On the other end of the spectrum, I would strongly recommend watching some good documentaries, because unlike a narrative film, details like the above example can’t be controlled. So everything becomes the job of the editor, to construct meaning, choose purposeful moments, etc.

I was incredibly taken by Undefeated, a 2011 documentary about a Memphis football team, how it created some very cinematic sequences via the use of editing. Even just the intro is so well cut that I immediately felt like I was in good hands.

And if you haven’t already seen it, watch F For Fake. Probably the most dazzling example of cinematic editing I’ve ever seen.

(I’ll also note the most innovative editing these days is actually online and in commercials, but not sure if you were interested in those.)
  - > No Other Choice  
  
The shovel shot, with his wife rolling over in bed. I saw the movie over a month ago and I’m still awestruck by it.
  - Came here to talk Park Chan Wook, his latest films have been on a whole other level of formal control.
2. Recently, I thought Nickel Boys had some very interesting editing and cinematography. Several scenes are intercut with films or important pivotal televised moments of the era. The entire thing really feels like entering someone’s memory.
3. If you haven't already, do watch Fritz Lang's Metropolis (1927). It seems like it often goes unnoticed, tbh, and is, dare I say it, underrated, compared to the other great films, like Battleship Potemkin (1925) (which you surely must have watched already).

Metropolis (1927), imo, is the single most ambitious work that belongs to the art of Cinema, in every single way. It features haunting visuals that stay with you long after you finish the film, an amazing score (it being a silent film), and an extremely unique style of editing, especially to eyes accustomed to modern films. Please do watch it, OP.
4. Lots of classic examples already listed but I wanted to pitch in *Don’t Look Now*, edited by Graeme Clifford but certainly with Nic Roeg involved, and the editing at the end in particular is astounding. Also it’s difficult and rash to avoid Godard here - the New Wave in general, but *Breathless* is hugely important and Godard kept himself and his audience on their toes throughout his career. Jean Cocteau also, and Buñuel. I think these are all directors who considered the edit as a fundamental part of their creative vision, equal to and inseparable from the sounds and visuals.
5. Peter Greenaways Prosperos books, multilayered imagery, txt written as dialog is spoken, multi split screen, so much going on.

The whole thing is up on [youtube](https://youtu.be/sgBT6tvP5pg?si=aIaw4dUV-m_sq6ee)
6. Catch-22 (1970) - every scene is connected to the following scene with an audio/visual marker. I believe every scene is one shot as well. I need to revisit the movie and write it all down. For instance the last word we hear in a scene is "knife" and the next scene opens with a closeup of a knife or a scene ends with a series of trucks moving from right to left then it cuts to series of something else moving from right to left, etc...
7. If you're digging into the history of it, you need to at least look at The Birth of a Nation. Yes, it's terrible KKK propaganda, but it's also the first time we see a vast majority of the language of editing appear (which also means that watching it will be a bit of a Seinfeld isn't Funny phenomenon, where it's hard to appreciate what it's doing, because everything it's doing is very standard now).
8. I just re-watched Everything Everywhere All At Once, and boy is the editing in that movie perfect. The balancing of so many alternate dimensions, but in a way where you're never totally confused about where you are in the story, is phenomenal. And it honestly wouldn't surprise me if they used a higher framerate for some sequences just so they could cut more than once per "frame."

Wong Kar Wai's films have this romantic quality to them, and his edits make them feel incredibly ephemeral and dream-like. Chungking Express and In The Mood For Love are his two most accessible works. 

Satoshi Kon worked in animation because he wanted to do things that only animation could do. A lot of that comes through in his edits -- absolutely *wild* transitions, jumping through time and space, few-frame inserts... Kon stretched the medium to its limit.

Tarsem's The Fall needs to be mentioned [just based on this transition alone.](https://youtu.be/f-lHb4zjC_k?si=G62g9y4PWkQEF7fT)

Finally, all these are examples of editing a "lot", so I'd encourage you to watch the exact opposite as well. Yasujiro Ozu hardly ever moved his camera, and so he relies on editing (and incredible framing / blocking) to define his scenes and settings in space. He's considered a genius for a reason.

## 4. Christopher Guest as an auteur

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Today I'd like to start a thread about someone I think deserves more respect discussion as an interesting, idiosyncratic auteur filmmaker. 

Christopher Guest directed seven feature films. Except for his debut The Big Picture and Almost Heroes, a "one for them" gig as a director for hire, Guest's films have a very consistent style. They're mockumentaries, and **Christopher Guest** and **mockumentary** are about as synonymous as **Hitchcock** and **suspense** or **Disney** and **animation**.

Guest uses a very different process than almost any other filmmaker. He famously doesn't use scripts. Instead, Guest and his collaborators create a basic scene outline and leave it completely up to the actors to develop characterization and improvise dialogue. He shoots dozens and dozens of hours of footage which is whittled down into the final film over an extensive editing process that can take over a year.

It's a kind of filmmaking that requires a specific style of acting, and Guest assembled a regular ensemble of actors and actresses who show up in most of his movies: Eugene Levy, Bob Balaban, John Michael Higgins, Jane Lynch, Michael McKean, Catherine O'Hara, Parker Posey, Ed Begley Jr., Jennifer Coolidge, Harry Shearer and Fred Willard.

Thematically, I think there is a consistent idea across his core films (Waiting for Guffman, Best in Show, A Might Wind, For Your Consideration), which is that of a single event as a gathering place for a collection of eccentric people with no awareness about their special niche interest. Guest is somewhat of an Altmanesque filmmaker in his focus on ensembles rather than single protagonists. And while his films do poke fun at the worlds of community theater and dog shows and folk music, I think they also kind of celebrate those worlds as communities of odd people who would be outsiders in most places.

I understand why he's underestimated or even underrated as a director. His commitment to the faux-documentary style really limits what he can do in terms of cinematography or mise-en-scene. He's never ventured out of his signature style to do a completely different film in a completely different style. 

But I think the fact that he has such a signature style (and one that was quite influential; without him, there's no The Office or Parks & Rec) is a strong claim to auteur status. What do you think?

    - "Auteur" isn't some elite status for conferring greater artistic value on someone's work. It's literally just a word for describing directors who maintain a strong authorial voice in their work and usually have a consistent style or themes. Michael Bay and Neil Breen are every bit the auteurs that Hitchcock and Scorcese are.

## 5. The last frame of Mouse Hunt (1997) is the entire plot of Ratatouille (2007)

**Score:** 7
**Body:** Mouse Hunt ends with Nathan Lane, now a successful chef, with a regular-sized mouse perched on his shoulder taste-testing his food. Ten years later, Pixar releases Ratatouille — the same image expanded into a full movie.

I can't find any evidence that Brad Bird or anyone at Pixar has ever mentioned Mouse Hunt. Neither has anyone involved with Mouse Hunt claimed credit. Maybe it's convergent evolution — dirty mice and clean kitchens are an obvious comedic juxtaposition.

But it's hard for me to believe that Ratatouille exists without that specific and bizarre Mouse Hunt ending. The image is too particular: not just "mouse in a kitchen" but "mouse wearing chef hat as secret collaborator in a world where mice are not magical".

Last week the White House posted a photoshop of Trump and a penguin walking toward icy mountains — a clear reference to the "deranged penguin" sequence from Encounters at the End of the World, where Herzog films a penguin marching away from its colony toward certain death in the interior, narrating about insanity and derangement. Other administration accounts posted penguin content that made the Herzog connection explicit.

So what is the meaning of the connection? With Mouse Hunt/Ratatouille, if it's intentional, why has no one ever said so? If it's coincidence, what does it mean that the same image emerged twice? With Herzog/Trump, the reference seems deliberate — but it's hardly a flattering comparison. Why do the Trump posters see it as positive?

Maybe framing it in terms of "posters" and "filmmakers" is wrong, and it's just the images themselves churning around?

I've got images and video clips [in a post here](https://ctxwindow.link/herzog-penguins).
